column2: Spiritual coaching combines coaching techniques with wisdom traditions and somatic (body-based) practices. It's not therapy, and it's not one religion — it's a personalized exploration of what spirituality, purpose, and authenticity mean for you.

column1: Do I need to be religious or have existing beliefs?
column2: No. I work with people across all beliefs — atheists, agnostics, devout practitioners of any faith. Spiritual coaching is about your personal truth, not any doctrine.

column1: What can I realistically expect to shift?
column2: Most people experience clarity within the first few sessions — they stop feeling lost. Over weeks and months: released patterns, deeper peace, better boundaries, alignment with their values, and a sense of purpose that radiates into every part of their life.

column1: How is this different from therapy?
column2: Therapy often focuses on healing past wounds and managing symptoms. Coaching focuses on moving forward — discovering who you want to become and building the inner foundation to live that. Many people do both.
Healthy skepticism is good — it keeps us grounded. A free discovery call lets you feel my energy and approach. No pressure, no sales pitch. If it doesn't resonate, that's okay.
Completely confidential. Our conversations, your practices, your journey — all held in sacred privacy.

column1: The Art of Letting Go: Release What No Longer Serves
column3: Master the spiritual practice of releasing patterns, beliefs, and attachments that limit your freedom and authentic expression.
column4:
One of the most transformative spiritual practices is learning what to release.

We spend much of our lives accumulating: beliefs, habits, relationships, identities. But growth often demands the opposite — the willingness to let go.

Why Letting Go Is Hard

Letting go feels like loss, even when what we're releasing causes us pain. We cling to the familiar because at least we know it. The unknown terrifies us.

The Practice of Sacred Release

I use a simple somatic practice with clients: notice what you're holding (literally and metaphorically), feel the weight of it, speak what you're releasing, then consciously open your hands and let it fall away.

Freedom lives on the other side of release.

This practice works whether you're releasing a belief, a relationship, a version of yourself that no longer fits, or even unexpressed grief.

After the Release

Notice the space that opens. That's where your new self is born.
column10: Woman in meditation posture releasing into surrender

column1: Finding Your North Star: How to Reconnect With Purpose
column3: A guided exploration of discovering (or rediscovering) your authentic purpose and the spiritual practice of staying aligned with it.
column4:
Purpose is not something you find once and keep forever. It evolves as you do.

Many of my clients come in burnt out, wondering "Is this really what I'm meant to do?" They've disconnected from their north star — the inner knowing of why they're here.

Purpose vs. Goals

Goals are destinations. Purpose is why the journey matters.

You can achieve every goal and still feel empty if you've lost sight of purpose. Conversely, you can pursue a purpose-driven life and feel fulfilled even when external circumstances are imperfect.

Reconnecting With Your Why

Here's a practice: sit quietly and ask yourself: "What makes me feel most alive? What would I do even without external reward?" Listen. Don't think. Feel.

Your answer is a thread back to your north star.

column1: Grief as a Gateway: Honoring Loss in Your Spiritual Unfolding
column3: Why grief is not the enemy of spirituality — it's an invitation to deeper compassion, wisdom, and wholeness.
column4:
Spiritual seekers sometimes believe they should transcend difficult emotions like grief. But this is a misunderstanding.

Grief is sacred. It's how we honor what mattered. And it opens us to profound compassion — for ourselves and others.

Grief Isn't Linear

It comes in waves. You can be "healed" and still cry. This is wholeness, not regression.

The Spiritual Work of Grieving

Instead of rushing past it, I invite clients to befriend their grief. Sit with it. Journal with it. Let it move through your body. It has wisdom for you.

On the Other Side

When you stop fighting your grief, you find softness. Wisdom. A deeper capacity to love.
